metadata.title: Elite Dangerous Exobiology Species Database
metadata.purpose: Reference data to identify exobiology species present on a planet and evaluate financial gain from sampling them.
sources: https://canonn.science/codex/ (Canonn Research Group, cross-referenced for gravity/temperature/volcanism precision)

field_notes.value_credits: Base Vista Genomics sale value in credits. 'First Logged' bonus on Vista Genomics multiplies this (commonly 4-5x) for the first sample of a species from a given body.
field_notes.gravity: Surface gravity range (in G) within which the species has been recorded; max_g is the effective spawn ceiling used for planning.
field_notes.min_distance_between_genetic_samples_m: Minimum colonial separation: the distance a commander must move between Genetic Sampler scans to register a second/third distinct sample of the same species.
field_notes.temperature_k: Recorded planetary mean surface temperature range (Kelvin) within which the species has been found. 'N/A'-type genera with very wide tolerance show the full recorded spread.
field_notes.location: Planetary body type(s) on which the species is regularly found (e.g. Rocky, High Metal Content, Icy, Rocky Ice).

species.description: A Recepta species where the body of the organism is suspended above ground inside a sphere-shaped translucent membrane filled with chemical-rich fluid that protects the organism and provides the chemical soup needed for metabolism.

A Recepta species that produces a thick lattice of trunks in a deltahedron shape, growing around and above the globular central organism to capture, retain and focus geothermal heat for thermosynthesis.

A Recepta species that grows a thick latticed structure for protection. A fine translucent membrane stretches between its gaps, allowing sunlight to penetrate and reach the inner organism for photosynthesis.
species.location: Rocky, High Metal Content, Icy, Rocky Ice

species.description: A Bacterial species that converts energy from neon-based atmospheres, creating looping whirls of bright colour.
A Bacterial species found in ammonia-based atmospheres that lives in sunlight; a colony's appearance resembles an intricate maze.
These Bacteria thrive on sunlight in carbon dioxide atmospheres, causing blanket coloration across a planetary surface.
This species of Bacteria thrives on atmospheric methane, appearing as a network of linked bubble patterns.
species.location: High Metal Content, Icy, Rocky, Rocky Ice

species.description: A sunlight-converting Bacterial species on worlds with atmospheres dominated by water and sulphur dioxide; colonies resemble a brain-shaped mass of smaller connected cells.
species.location: High Metal Content, Rocky, Rocky Ice

species.description: This species of Bacteria can be found in nitrogen atmospheres and forms a shapeless mass across the surface.
species.location: High Metal Content, Icy, Rocky, Rocky Ice

species.description: This Bacterial species survives exclusively on atmospheric helium on icy bodies; distinguished by a radial pattern extending outward from the colony's centre.
This species of Bacteria converts geothermal heat from nitrogen- or ammonia-based volcanic sites into energy, appearing as long interlinked strands across the surface.

species.volcanism: Carbon Dioxide Geysers or Minor Methane Magma
species.description: This species of Bacteria thrives on the heat generated by carbon- or methane-based volcanic activity, appearing as long swirling ridges on the surface.

species.volcanism: Minor Nitrogen/Methane magma; Water magma/geysers; Silicate/Water geysers
species.description: This highly atmosphere-adaptive species of Bacteria appears as an intricate web pattern, thriving near most magma and geyser volcanic activity, but also found on bodies with no volcanism.
This species of Bacteria appears as an intricate web pattern, showing a preference for exclusively water-based volcanic activity.
This species of Bacteria survives on worlds with argon atmospheres and appears as a collection of tight loops on the ground.
species.location: High Metal Content, Icy, Rocky, Rocky Ice

species.description: This species of Bacteria is dependent upon oxygen atmospheres and creates random swirling patterns across the ground.
species.location: High Metal Content, Icy, Rocky, Rocky Ice

species.description: These Fonticulua thrive in argon atmospheres and can reach four metres in height, featuring huge leaf-like structures to capture sunlight for conversion to energy.
A Fonticulua species that thrives in methane-based atmospheres, sprouting a cluster of cylindrical tubes directly from the ice.
A species of Fonticulua that exists on worlds with oxygen atmospheres, producing coiling wave-shaped structures which tilt toward sunlight.
A Fonticulua species that exists in atmospheres with a heavy concentration of nitrogen. Bright gem-like pods grow along the main stalk and can break off to create new colonies.
A species of Fonticulua found in atmospheres dominated by neon, appearing as a pyramid-shaped cluster of frilled sections.
This Fonticulua species can be found on ice worlds with argon-rich atmospheres, producing broad hoop-shaped structures to better reflect weak sunlight onto themselves for photosynthesis.
This Frutexa species has vivid colouration when young that alters as it matures; its upper branches produce lines of small pea-like seed pods.
A species of Frutexa characterised by its spiky lower branches surrounding a thick central column, which is clotted with spores and has a dark crown.
This species of Frutexa combines broad branches with long thin stalks, along which grow clusters of lightweight seed pods that are scattered by light winds.
A species of Frutexa that appears as a bush of leaves with a texture similar to seaweed; seeds are extended on long stalks protected by a cage formation until ready to germinate.
A Frutexa species that gives the appearance of flames, with vivid upright fronds extended from multiple stalks dotted with disc-shaped spores distributed by the wind.
This species of Frutexa has an almost metallic shine to its small leaves; spherical spores with star-shaped openings grow along its upper branches.
A Frutexa species that produces clusters of upright intertwining branches crowned with bright seed sacks.
A Stratum species with a vaguely octopoid shape; pale semi-translucent upper domes reveal colourful inner organisms contrasting with darker outstretched tentacles.
A Stratum species that displays fleshy ovoid shapes connected in a narrow pattern across the ground, covered with streaks of round photosynthetic cells that absorb sunlight.
This Stratum species appears as a mixture of tight concentric ring patterns and mottled proto-leaves in a mixture of dark hues.
This species of Stratum forms broad interconnected ring structures composed of narrow ridges to capture sunlight.
This particular Stratum species gives the appearance of overlapping rock plateaus, each with narrow bands of colouration.
This species of Stratum appears as a series of unconnected ovoid shapes across the ground, the protruding tips of a larger subterranean organism.
A Stratum that blends thick overlapping vines with irregular growths, with varying colours appearing in bands or streaks.

species.atmosphere: Ammonia, Carbon Dioxide, Sulphur Dioxide, Water
species.description: A Stratum species with a thick rock-like outer shell, covered with an irregular lattice of brighter cells that absorb sunlight for photosynthesis.
A Tussock species characterised by leaves with a distinctive striped pattern that are bisected like a snake's tongue; mature versions sprout smaller spore-producing leaves.
This Tussock species is a squat cluster of leaves resembling thick matted hair, from which sprout thick pods carrying a number of round beans.
A Tussock species with leaves that have a thick segmented lower half and a willowy upper half; mature versions produce separate spore-clotted ovoid organisms on stalks.
This species of Tussock has very thin stalks carrying twin sets of seed sacks along their entire length, resembling links on a chain.
A Tussock species with tall sharp reeds reaching about two metres, characterised by narrow markings along their length.
This Tussock species blends thick segmented lower growths with longer, narrower leaves; mature versions have pale spores along the upper branches.
This Tussock species produces thick intertwined leaves, above which sprout narrow stems crowned with seed pods.
A Tussock species that extends large seed pods on thin stems above a cluster of bright leaves.
A Tussock species with feather-shaped growths surrounding a single segmented stem, which when mature is crowned with colourful seeds.
A Tussock species that sprouts tapering leaves, with tips covered with colourful seed pods.
This Tussock species sprouts serrated leaves around thick stalks that produce dark seed pods.
This Tussock species resembles a patch of tough, wiry grasses; taller stalks carrying disc-shaped seed pods rise above the main organism when mature.
A Tussock species with thin tough leaves marked with dark stripes, from which sprout taller stalks with small leaves that release seeds to the winds.
A Tussock species that blends tough lower stalks with taller willowy reeds, which produce small pale spores.
A Tussock species with thin reeds clustered around a central stalk, which is eventually crowned with spores.
A Fungoida that features clusters of mottled bubble-shaped growths atop a central stalk, containing spores that can be exposed to the wind for distribution.
This Fungoida species resembles an upturned jellyfish, emerging from a solid base buried within the substrate; fleshy reproductive tissue can float on the breeze to form new organisms.
This Fungoida species produces vertical clusters interspersed with spore pods atop thin stalks, allowing them to break off and scatter to reproduce elsewhere.
species.location: High Metal Content, Rocky, Rocky Ice

species.description: This Fungoida species produces vertical clusters interspersed with spore pods atop thin stalks, allowing them to break off and scatter to reproduce elsewhere.
An Osseus species that produces a stacked series of spiral structures up to about three metres in height; ridged features are upturned to better absorb sunlight for photosynthesis.
An Osseus species that appears as half-buried discs with radial patterns, resembling natural rock formations from a distance; absorbs geothermal energy from below and heat sources above.
species.location: High Metal Content, Rocky, Rocky Ice

species.description: This Osseus species can grow to over six metres across, producing wide ridged frills for metabolic interactions including absorbing sunlight for energy production.
A species of Osseus with a single broad stalk from which extend wide circular structures, with the largest plate capping the top to maximise sunlight absorption.
An Osseus species that grows a single thick stalk from which emerges a wide, broadly circular, pitted endoskeleton designed to dramatically increase surface area for chemosynthesis.
species.location: High Metal Content, Rocky, Rocky Ice

species.description: A species of Osseus that produces coiling spiral structures up to six metres wide, with ridged folds on their upturned surfaces designed to capture sunlight.
This Aleoida species has upright clumps of long serrated leaves, which can open up to expose a reproductive organ containing tiny round seeds.
This Aleoida species has an interleaved crown of mottled leaves that can grow to head height, with explosive seed pods emerging on long protruding stalks.
This Aleoida species has wide flat leaves on a heavy bark base and can reach huge sizes, sprouting a dome-shaped reproductive organ at its peak.
This Aleoida species has a circle of upturned leaves marked with patterns, surrounding a bright fleshy pod with darker markings which matures in its centre.
This Aleoida species features long spiky leaves that can reach over two metres high surrounding a single reproductive organ on a long central stalk.

species.atmosphere: Ammonia, Argon, Nitrogen, Oxygen, Sulphur Dioxide, Water
species.volcanism: Major/Minor Water Geysers, Major/Minor Water Magma
species.description: A species of Fumerola found near sites of water-based volcanic activity, appearing as small dark clusters with ridged folds that trap heat within.

species.volcanism: Minor Carbon Dioxide Geysers, Minor Methane Magma
species.description: A Fumerola species found near sites of carbon-based volcanism, appearing as a thin upright tube; an inner organism protrudes from an opening at its peak to increase heat absorption.

species.volcanism: Major/Minor Silicate Vapour Geysers; Major/Minor Rocky or Metallic Magma
species.description: This Fumerola species has a preference for particulate-based volcanism types, appearing as long vertical stalks with smaller fronds that can stretch out.
species.location: High Metal Content, Rocky, Rocky Ice

species.description: This species of Fumerola prefers nitrogen-based volcanism, producing an ovoid organism with dotted markings that sits on top of a thin stalk.
A species of Electricae that extends a tip of four connected loops above the ice, each covered with brightly luminous fronds; most typically found near Stellar Classes A, White Dwarf, or Neutron.
This Electricae species protrudes bioluminescent stalks that radiate out in all directions; evidence links this species to the proximity of nebulae.
These Concha are found on worlds with nitrogen-based atmospheres; their rounded rock-like structure splits apart to extend long stalks topped with loops.
This Concha species resembles a ridged, bisected egg until it cracks in half, allowing a thin stalk to sprout from its fleshy insides, covered with doughnut-shaped pods for chemical exchange.
A Concha species that thrives in atmospheres rich with carbon dioxide; the lip-like upper opening cracks apart to allow a vertical growth of spiky leaves and bright seeds to stretch upward.
A species of Concha that relies on heat sources to survive; as the bisected growth increases in size it sprouts a single stalk topped with an array of luminous fronds that facilitate metabolism.
A species of Cactoida that can reach over three metres in height, composed of multiple growths that sprout sealed pods at their peaks, which open up to distribute seeds.
This species of Cactoida appears as a squat growth with a latticed upper surface, which eventually produces a cluster of seed pods.
This Cactoida species appears as a swollen five-sided growth, reaching over two metres high and topped with an intersected crown. Only found in ammonia atmospheres.
This species of Cactoida, exclusively found in carbon dioxide atmospheres, has a globular base from which extend vertical cylinders that can reach over four metres, with rounded seed pods.
This Cactoida species appears as a tall collection of cylinders linked by an undulating membrane and topped with a spiky crown; the most heat-tolerant of Cactoida species.
A species of Clypeus that grows a broad, tear-shaped shield to protect the sensitive organism from extreme sunlight; the shield's ridges help direct water droplets down into the soil.
This Clypeus species produces a curved shield that resembles a large pearl in shape and texture; up to three central organisms grow within it upon a supporting bed of leaves.
A Clypeus species that grows an angular shield with a mirrored exterior to protect the spiky organisms inside. Found greater than 2,500 Ls from the parent star.
A Tubus species that extends pale vertical stalks composed of rigid modules; colourful fronds frequently appear in the gaps between segments and aid with controlling gaseous exchange.
A Tubus species with narrow pale segments and fronds growing between each module; a wide crown of leaves at the peak holds spores on their undersides to germinate across a wide area.
A Tubus species formed from hollow vertical cylinders that can reach heights of six metres; mature specimens are capped with a downturned crown that can distribute seeds on the wind.
This Tubus species is composed of squat tubes growing into a vertical spire; the upper pods of mature specimens produce explosive seed pods on their outer skin.
This species of Tubus grows a cluster of hollow stalks composed of rigid segments; over time these become capped with a growth that flowers and produces seeds.
